distractor_rationales: This flips the sign inside the parentheses, adding \( \cos([[n]]\pi) \) instead of subtracting it from 1.
This keeps only the \( \cos([[n]]\pi) \) term, forgetting the leading 1 from evaluating at \( x=0 \).
This multiplies by \( [[n]] \) instead of dividing by it.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.

solution: For an ODD power, \( \sin^{[[m]]}(x) \) is handled by substituting \( u=\cos x \) (splitting off one factor of \( \sin x \)); over \( [0,\pi/2] \) this always yields a clean RATIONAL value (unlike even powers, which give a multiple of \( \pi \)). Here \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\sin^{[[m]]}(x)\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly applies the EVEN-power Wallis result (a multiple of \( \pi \)) to this odd power.
This divides by the exponent instead of applying the correct odd-power reduction.
This multiplies by the exponent instead of applying the correct odd-power reduction.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

solution: By the Wallis reduction formula, an EVEN power of cosine integrated over \( [0,\pi/2] \) always yields a rational multiple of \( \pi \) (unlike odd powers, which give a clean rational number with no \( \pi \)). Here \( \int_{0}^{\pi/2} [[c]]\cos^{[[m]]}(x)\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by the exponent instead of applying the correct Wallis reduction.
This multiplies by the exponent instead of applying the correct Wallis reduction.
This wrongly uses the \( m=2 \) result (\( \pi/4 \), scaled by \( c \)) regardless of the actual exponent.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

solution: Using the product-to-sum identity \( \sin([[m]]x)\cos([[n]]x) = \tfrac12\left[\sin(([[m]]+[[n]])x) + \sin(([[m]]-[[n]])x)\right] \) and integrating each sine term over \( [0,\pi] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly overgeneralizes the sine/cosine ORTHOGONALITY result (which gives 0 for \( \sin(mx)\sin(nx) \) or \( \cos(mx)\cos(nx) \) with \( m\ne n \)) to this mixed sine-cosine product, which is generally nonzero.
This evaluates the integrand at \( x=\pi \) instead of integrating it.
This is a leftover value from the wrong even-power Wallis pattern, unrelated to this product integral.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is half of the correct value.
This is double the correct value.

solution: Since \( \dfrac{d}{dx}\tan x = \sec^{2}x \), an antiderivative of \( \sec^{2}x \) is \( \tan x \). Evaluating at the endpoints, \( \tan(\arctan([[q]])) - \tan(\arctan([[p]])) = [[q]]-[[p]] \), so the integral is \( [[c]]\left([[q]]-[[p]]\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the subtraction order, giving the negative of the correct value.
This forgets to multiply by the coefficient \( [[c]] \).
This mistakenly applies a power-rule antiderivative (as if integrating \( x \)) instead of \( \sec^{2}x \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by \( [[c]] \) from the correct value.
This is half of the correct value.
